coredata.dc:title: The preparation of chiral carbon dots and the study on their antibacterial abilities
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Xu, Mengyu
Dai, Kang
Liu, Huan
Jiao, Yan
Xiao, Xincai
coredata.dc:description: Chirality is one of the most widespread properties in nature. After the successful application of non-optical active carbon dots (CDs) in the fields of detection, imaging, and antibacterial, the researchers turned more attention to the preparation and applications of chiral CDs. This paper reports the preparation of several chiral CDs with chiral amino acids as carbon sources through two different methods (one-step hydrothermal route and two-step coupling route), and further explores the difference in antibacterial abilities of these chiral CDs prepared by different carbon sources and synthesis methods. The experiments results showed that chiral D/LGU which are prepared through one-step method and with glutamic acid (Glu), are superior to those prepared by two-step method (NLDU/NDGU), or those prepared with cysteine as raw material (D/LCS). For D/LCS, the antibacterial ability of the L type is superior to that of the D type, proving that the synthesis method and the chirality of raw material are both important in the antibacterial behavior of prepared CDs. The chirality and optical properties of prepared CDs have been characterized by UV–vis spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrology, and circular dichroism. Through MurA enzyme inhibition tests, SEM, and molecular docking design, the antibacterial mechanism of chiral CDs has been investigated. The research provides a sound foundation for the preparation, characterization, and application of chiral CDs, as well as the novel targeting antibacterial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chirality
Carbon dots
Antibacterial activity
Enzyme inhibition
Molecular docking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mass expression of E. coli MurA recombinant proteins
E. coli MurA purification of recombinant proteins
Determination of the enzymatic activity of E. coli MurA
Antibacterial activity and antibacterial mechanism of chiral CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis spectra and PL emission of (a) DGU, (c) N-CDs and (e) NDGU. Insets: images of the CDs solutions under daylight and UV light at 365 nm wavelength. The fluorescence intensity under different excitation of (b) DGU, (d) N-CDs and (f) NDGU.
Circular dichroism spectra for (a) L/D-Glu, (b) L/DGU, and (c) NLGU/NDGU.
TEM images of (a) DGU and (b) NDGU. Inset: the top left image is a lattice diagram of CDs, the histogram of the particle size distribution of CDs is shown in the lower right corner.
FT-IR spectra of (a) D/LGU and (b) NLGU/NDGU.
XPS spectra of a survey scan of DGU, (a) XPS survey scan (b) C 1s of DGU, (c) N 1s of DGU, (d) O 1s of DGU, (e) XRD curve of DGU.
Cell viability in the presence of (a) L/DGU and (b) NLGU/NDGU were detected using the cck-8 kit. Error bars indicate the relative deviation of cell viability in the six wells.
The zeta potential of (a) D/LGU and (b) NLGU/NDGU.
SEM images of S. aureus after treatment with (a) PBS and (b) DGU (1 mg/mL).
(a) MurA enzyme inhibition by four types of CDs. (b) Inhibitory effect of Fosfomvcin Sodium on MurA. The error bar is the deviation when measuring three times.
(a) and (c) are schematic diagram of molecular docking between DGU and MurA. (b) and (d) are schematic diagram of molecular docking between LGU and MurA.
Schematic diagram of the antibacterial mechanism of action of the six kinds of CDs.
The antimicrobial ability of different CDs for different bacteria.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: G+: Gram-positive bacteria G−: Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of antibacterial activity of DGU with other chiral CDs.
Docking results of D/LGU with MurA enzyme.

head.ce:title: The preparation of chiral carbon dots and the study on their antibacterial abilities

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan, 430074, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University), Wuhan, 430065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University), Wuhan 430065, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan, 430074, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chirality is one of the most widespread properties in nature. After the successful application of non-optical active carbon dots (CDs) in the fields of detection, imaging, and antibacterial, the researchers turned more attention to the preparation and applications of chiral CDs. This paper reports the preparation of several chiral CDs with chiral amino acids as carbon sources through two different methods (one-step hydrothermal route and two-step coupling route), and further explores the difference in antibacterial abilities of these chiral CDs prepared by different carbon sources and synthesis methods. The experiments results showed that chiral D/LGU which are prepared through one-step method and with glutamic acid (Glu), are superior to those prepared by two-step method (NLDU/NDGU), or those prepared with cysteine as raw material (D/LCS). For D/LCS, the antibacterial ability of the L type is superior to that of the D type, proving that the synthesis method and the chirality of raw material are both important in the antibacterial behavior of prepared CDs. The chirality and optical properties of prepared CDs have been characterized by UV–vis spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrology, and circular dichroism. Through MurA enzyme inhibition tests, SEM, and molecular docking design, the antibacterial mechanism of chiral CDs has been investigated. The research provides a sound foundation for the preparation, characterization, and application of chiral CDs, as well as the novel targeting antibacterial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality specificity widely exists in the biochemical processes of biological systems, such as uptake, sensing, synthesis, and metabolism, and chirality is one of the critical biochemical characteristics for biological systems [1]. Thanks to the excellent water-solubility [2], adjustable light emission [3], dispersion, low toxicity, and satisfactory biocompatibility [4,5] of CDs, which have become the focus of research interests for nanomaterials [6,7]. The combination of chirality with the excellent properties of CDs would greatly promise the bright outlook for functional CDs in biochemical fields.

Compared with traditional non-optical active CDs, chiral CDs show promising applications in catalysis [8,9], regulating enzyme activity [10–12], chiral substance detection [13,14], antibacterial [15,16], and anticancer [17]. For instance, Liu et al. obtained chiral CDs through one-step hydrothermal route with citric acid and d-proline as precursors, and the product showed high asymmetric catalytic activity for enantioselective direct aldol condensation [18]. Wang and his team designed and prepared chiral CDs (L/D-Trp-CDs) with citric acid and L/D-tryptophan as raw materials, and the prepared L-Trp-CDs has been used to adjust the activity of laccase with laccase inhibition rate at 87.26%, obviously higher than that of D-Trp-CDs (57.84%) prepared under same preparation environment [19]. Gao and his team used citric acid and L-Asp to prepare chrial CDs, and successfully applied them in the recognition of Lys enantiomers [20].

However, the researches on the chiral CDs’ applications in antibacterial and antitumor fields are still limited. In terms of the anti-cancer area, Xie and his team prepared D/L-CDs with citric acid and D/l-glutamic acid as raw materials and then prepared chiral nano vaccines (L/D-OVA) with ovalbumin (OVA) as antigen model, which can greatly inhibit the growth of melanoma [17].

In antibacterial fields, more research focused on the antibacterial ability of chiral nanomaterials [21], rather than that of chiral CDs. For instance, Chen et al. prepared sea cucumber-shaped chiral gold nano bipyramids (GBPs) and applied them in treating bacteria-caused infections. They also discovered that the combination of D-GBPs with protein A of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) was 12.39 times stronger than that of L-GBPs, making D-GBPs further attach to the surface of bacteria to realize the antibacterial activity [22]. Wang and his team prepared chiral hexagonal superstructures (CoSS) by combining Co2+ and L/d-tartaric acid (Tar) ligands in different proportions and found that compared with L-CoSS, D-CoSS exhibited stronger antibacterial ability against S. aureus [23].

Compared with complicated chiral antibacterial materials, chiral antibacterial CDs possess merits like a simple synthesis process, low cost, and excellent biocompatibility. For example, Victoria and his team prepared chiral cysteine carbon dots through one-step microwave method with citric acid and D/l-cysteine as raw materials, and the product has been applied in antibacterial experiments. The prepared D-CDs and L-CDs could eliminate Escherichia coli (E. coli) at 2 mg/mL and 4 mg/mL respectively [24]. Though amino acids exhibit certain antibacterial abilities [25], their weak ability [26] requires high concentration to realize that purpose. Therefore, the combination of amino acids with antibacterial active substances is of great significance to prepare high-efficient antibacterial chiral CDs.

For now, no published literature has discussed the difference between chiral CDs in their antibacterial activities caused by the chiral source or synthesis route. Moreover, the exploration of the antibacterial mechanism is also critical for antibacterial materials. SEM was used to observe the impact of antibacterial materials upon the morphology of bacterial cells [27], and the detections of active oxygen, DNA, and proteins can be used to assess the damage [28] caused by the material. Molecular pairing can also be used to discuss the potential sites and binding in antibacterial mechanisms [29–31].

This paper reports the preparation of chiral CDs with several amino acids and the impacts of different preparation routes and chiral sources upon the chirality and antibacterial abilities of prepared CDs. UV–vis spectrology, fluorescence spectrology, and circular dichroism have been employed to study the optical properties of prepared chiral CDs, and FT-IR, XPS, and XRD were used to analyze the elemental composition and surface group of the products. Meanwhile, common bacteria have been used to test the antibacterial ability of the prepared chiral CDs, and their antibacterial mechanism has been explored through enzyme inhibition experiments, SEM, and molecule paring.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW. 1800), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), l(−)-cysteine hydrochloride monohydrate (L-Cys), D-(+)-cysteine hydrochloride monohydrate (D-Cys), isopropyl-β-d-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG), d-glucose-6-phosphate disodium salt (UNAG), phosphoenol form pyruvate (PEP) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd.. D-(+)-glucose monohydrate (Glc, ≥99.5%), citric acid (CA, ≥85.0%), and sodium chloride were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.. l-Glutamic acid (L-Glu) from Bioharp Reagent Co., Ltd. and d-Glutamic acid (D-Glu), ammonium molybdate from Source Leaf Biological Co., Ltd.. 3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-Thiazolyl)-2,5-Diphenyl Tetrazolium Bromide (MTT) was purchased from Angus Chemical Co., Ltd.. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) hydrochloride and N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd..

In this experiment, Glu-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. The synthesis steps are as follows: 0.3 g CA, 0.2 g D/L-Glu and 0.2 g PEI1800 were added to 5 mL water, stir until dissolved, and after mixing evenly, transfer the mixed solution to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene stainless steel autoclave, and react at 160 °C for 1 h. The obtained light-yellow solution was centrifuged with a 3000 D ultrafiltration tube for 20 min to remove unreacted small molecules in the solution. The resulting solution was freeze-dried, and finally the desired solid was obtained. The resulting D-Glu-CDs were named DGU, while the resulting L-Glu-CDs were named LGU.

Besides, we also synthesized N-CDs-D-Glu/N-CDs-L-Glu using a two-step method. Based on previous experience in our laboratory, we synthesized the N-CDs [32]. 100 mg EDC and NHS were added to 3 mL of 50 mg/mL N-CDs solution, after stirring for 30 min, added 100 mg of D/L-Glu and 1 mL NaOH to dissolve it, then in N2 environment to stir for 10 min, and then stir for 24 h in room temperature. The stirred solution was placed in a 1000 D ultrafiltration tube for purification, and the obtained liquid was freeze-dried to obtain a solid. The resulting N-CDs-D-Glu were named NDGU, while the resulting N-CDs-L-Glu are named NLGU.
For subsequent exploration, we also synthesized Cys-CDs using the same method used to synthesize Glu-CDs again, the obtained L-Cys-CDs were named LCS, and the obtained D-Cys-CDs were named DCS.

Cell viability of mouse BV2 cells was determined using the cck-8 assay when D/LGU and NDCU/NLGU were present. BV2 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well, 100 μL of culture medium was added, and the cells were placed in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h for cell culture, ensuring that the cells spread evenly over the upper surface of the plates. The appropriate amounts of D/LGU and NDGU/NLGU were dissolved in saline and diluted with DMEM to the desired concentrations, and 100 μL of diluted samples of different concentrations were added to each well. Cells not treated with D/LGU and NDGU/NLGU were used as controls. The cells were incubated in a humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. The supernatant was discarded and 100 μL of cck-8 was added, incubated in the incubator for 30 min and the OD was measured at 450 nm using an enzyme marker. Cell viability was calculated as following(1) [33].
(1) Cell viability (%) = ODtreated / ODcontrol × 100%
where ODtreated and ODcontrol were optical density of cells in the presence and absence of D/LGU and NDGU/NLGU.

The Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were cultured in the Mueller-Hinton (MH) broth. The Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) were grown in Lennox broth (LB) medium. Incubate overnight at 37 °C (180 rpm). To judge the bacterial concentration, we measured the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) by UV–vis spectrophotometry.

The MIC of CDs was determined by the microdilution method using 96-well cell culture plates. The overnight bacterial cell culture was adjusted to a concentration of 2 × 107 CFU/mL. Each well contained 50 μL of bacterial cell suspension and 50 μL of different concentrations of D/LGU, NLGU/NDGU, and D/LCS, with a final volume of 100 μL/well. The treated bacterial samples were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. At the end of the incubation, 100 μL of the suspension was co-incubated with 5 μL of MTT for an additional 20 min. The formed precipitate was dissolved in DMSO for visual observation. The experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mass expression of E. coli MurA recombinant proteins
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL of Kanamycin was added to 200 mL LB liquid medium, and then PET26b-E. coli was incubated at 37 °C using a medium added to Kanamycin for 3 h. Then 200 μL of IPTG was added to it and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h, thus inducing the expression of E. coli MurA protein. Bacteria were collected by centrifugation at 4000×g for 20 min at 4 °C. The resulting organisms were resuspended using 1 x PBS and placed on ice, and the resulting organisms were sonicated using an ultrasonic crusher (12 min in total). The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: E. coli MurA purification of recombinant proteins
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli MurA recombinant protein was purified from the supernatant using His-Ni2+ affinity chromatography. After loading the packing, the chromatographic column is first equilibrated using the equilibration solution. After equilibration for 15 min, the prepared E. coli MurA recombinant protein was slowly sampled, allowing the recombinant protein to affinity with the chromatography column. At the end of the loading sample, the non-specific proteins bound to the substrate are first eluted using a washing solution. The column is finally washed with an eluent to elute the E. coli MurA target protein bound to the chromatographic column matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the enzymatic activity of E. coli MurA
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the catalytic reaction of enzymes, we can learn that after the enzymatic reaction, free phosphoric acid will be generated, and ammonium molybdate can effectively combine with phosphoric acid to form a phosphomolybdic acid complex, which can also combine with the color developer malachite green, making the color of malachite green change from yellow-green to blue-green. Therefore, this method can be used to detect the production or presence of phosphoric acid. The reaction catalyzed by MurA enzyme is：
UDP-GlcNAc + PEP↔UDP-Glc-NAc-EP + Pi

In a 96-well plate, 10 μL of ddH2O, UNAG, and MurA were added sequentially and then incubated for 5 min at 37 °C, followed by the addition of different concentrations of samples to be tested and PEP, and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. After the reaction was terminated by adding 50 μL of malachite green-ammonium molybdate color developer, the reaction was incubated for 20 min at 37 °C protected from light, and the OD value was measured at 620 nm using an enzyme marker.

Three-dimensional structure of E. coli MurA enzyme taken from PDB database (ID:1UAE). Molecular docking simulations are performed using the Surflex module in SYBYL. The structures of the proteins and DGU and LGU undergo the necessary preparation before molecular docking, and then molecular docking were to simulate the interaction of DGU and LGU with the MurA enzyme.

Two blue fluorescence-emitting chiral CDs (D/LGU) have been prepared through the one-step hydrothermal route with CA as reducing agent, PEI1800 as the passivating agent, D-Glu and L-Glu as carbon source respectively, and water as solvent (160 °C, 1 h). We have determined the basic optical properties of DGU and LGU by fluorescence and UV–vis (Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. S1(a)). Both CDs exhibit optimal absorption peaks at 360 nm, which is the result of the n-π* transition of C=O [34], and both optimal emission wavelength are at 450 nm. As shown in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1(b) and Fig. S1(c)) of both CDs under different excitation wavelength in the range of 320–400 nm, both CDs do not show excitation wavelength dependence. This may be caused by the even surface state of CDs acquired through the one-step hydrothermal route [35]. Similarly, D/L-Cys were used to replace D/L-Glu as the carbon source to prepare D/LCS, and the UV–vis spectra, fluorescence spectra, and circular dichroism of prepared CDs are shown in Fig. S2.

In the two-step synthesis, blue-green luminescent N-CDs were first prepared with CA as a reducing agent, PEI1800 as nitrogen source, and Glc as the carbon source at low temperature (80 °C), and then acquired NLGU/NDGU by coupling EDC/NHS (the combination of the carboxyl groups on Glu with the amino group on the surface of CDs). As shown in Fig. 1(c), (e) and Fig. S1(b), N-CDs, NLGU/NDGU all exhibit optimal absorption peaks at 360 nm with the optimal emission wavelength at 475 nm. Under the excitation of broad wavelength (320–340 nm), the emission wavelength of N-CDs show redshift from 473 nm to 496 nm (Fig. 1(d)), which hint that the fluorescence of prepared N-CDs was controlled by a heterogeneous emission state, while the wavelengths of NLGU/NDGU showed slight blueshift from 475 nm to 465 nm (Fig. 1(f)), which might be the result of poor structural uniformity of CDs during the synthesis process of two-step coupling method [36].

The quantum yields (QYs) of prepared D/LGU reached 30% (Table S1), while the QYs of NLGU/NDGU were much lower, at 6% and 5% respectively. The reason of this phenomenon is that during the process of the one-step route, the fully-reacted raw materials ensure uniform surface groups and rich existence of amino groups. For the two-step route, amino acids are wrapped on the surface of CDs, resulting in a large number of residual carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs, leading to surface defects, and thus low QYs [37].

Fig. S3(a), (b) show the intensities of chiral CDs prepared by two different synthesis routes under different pH conditions. The fluorescence intensity of D/LGU prepared by the one-step route was much lower in an acid or alkali environment than in neutral conditions, while for NDGU/NLGU, its fluorescence intensity remains stable when the pH value is in the range of 2–10. Fig. S3(c) shows that under the illumination of UV lamp (365 nm) for 0–30 min, both chiral CDs show relatively stable and similar fluorescence intensities, proving the excellent photostability of both chiral CDs.

The difference in the absorption of polarized light can help to determine the chirality of prepared CDs [38]. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), (b), the synthesized DGU/LGU had a reversible cotton effect at 210 nm, and the signal amplitude overlaps with the circular dichroic signal of the raw material Glu, indicating chirality in prepared DGU/LGU [39]. The circular dichroism spectrum of the DCS/LCS prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with D/L-Cys as raw material (Fig. S2(h)) shows two signal peaks at 210 nm and 260 nm, indicating the acquired CDs not only preserved chirality of Cys but also geted induced chirality in DCS/LCS core through chirality inheritance or transfer [40]. We thus believed that the chirality of CDs produced using one-step method comes from the chiral residue on the surface as well as from chiral compounds involved in the synthesis of carbon cores [41]. On the other hand, when D/L-Glu is coupled to the surface of N-CDs using two-step coupling procedure, as shown in Fig. 2(c), there exists a chiral signal on the surface of prepared CDs, and reversive signal at 210 nm, signifying the successful coupling. However, at the same concentration, the signal intensity of DGU/LGU is ten times stronger than that of NDGU/NLGU, demonstrating that chiral compounds can have more effective combinations and higher response values in one-step process. All of these findings supported the preparation of chiral CDs as successful.

To observe the morphology, particle size, distribution, surface functional groups, and elemental composition of the prepared CDs, we characterized them using TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), (b), the prepared CDs are in a spherical shape with excellent distribution and uniform size. The average particle size of DGU prepared through the one-step route is 2.32 nm, while that of NDGU prepared by the two-step method is 2.07 nm. The functional groups on the surface of CDs have been characterized by FT-IR. As seen in Fig. 4 (a), the characteristic peaks of C–H are located at 2842 cm−1, 2960 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1, while the characteristic peak of C=O appears at 1704 cm−1. The peak at 1280 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration peak of C–O, whereas the peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to the N–H and C–H vibrations, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the peak at 3270 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of NH2 and OH, while the peaks near 1580 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 represent the vibrations of N–H and C–H. The characteristic peaks of C–H appear at 2960 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1, and that of C=O appears at 1706 cm−1. From the result of FT-IR, it can be seen that the surface of CDs prepared through the one-step route contains abundant NH2, leading to high QYs of CDs, while the surface of CDs prepared by the two-step route contains a rich amount of OH and COOH, ensuring good water-solubility but decreased QYs.

There are three peaks in the XPS spectra of DGU (Fig. 5 (a)), corresponding to C, N and O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(b), there exist three BE peaks in C 1s spectra, corresponding to C=C (284.54 eV), C–O (285.76 eV), and C–N (287.63 eV) [42]. There are two BE peaks in the N 1s spectra in Fig. 5(c), which correspond to C–N=C (398.92 eV) and N–H (400.82 eV) [43]. In the spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 5(d)), the two peaks are for C=O (530.60 eV) and C–O (531.93 eV) [44]. We also investigated the crystallinity of the prepared CDs by XRD analysis. As seen in Fig. 5(e), the CDs show a broad diffraction peak between 2θ = 20° and 30°. It confirms that the prepared CDs have an amorphous structure [45].

We, therefore, used mouse BV2 neuronal cells for cytotoxicity testing of CDs via the cck-8 kit. Fig. 6 (a) shows the cells treated with D/LGU. After 24 h of incubation, the cell survival rate of both CDs was higher than that of the blank group because amino acids are the basic substances that constitute the proteins required for animal nutrition. However, the cell survival rate of DGU was lower than that of LGU, probably because D-Glu is a synthetic amino acid, while L-Glu is an essential amino acid in the organism, so the toxicity of DGU was slightly higher than that of LGU after synthesis of CDs. For the NLGU/NDGU experimental group, as shown in Fig. 6(b), the viability of cells treated with both CDs also reached more than 100%, producing a pro-growth phenomenon, indicating that both CDs are essentially non-cytotoxic. These experimental results demonstrate the low biological toxicity of these CDs and their suitability for future biochemical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity and antibacterial mechanism of chiral CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MIC has been regarded as the essential standard to assess the antibacterial ability of materials. The MICs of prepared D/LGU, NDGU/NLGU, and D/LCS have been determined to the antibacterial abilities of these CDs. The Gram-positive bacteria (G+) of S. aureus and B. subtilis, and the Gram-negative bacteria (G−) of E. coli and P. aeruginosa, were chosen as the test targets.

The cells were firstly cultured in 96 well plated and treated with D/LGU, NDGU/NLGU, and D/LCS at different concentrations, and the surviving bacteria in the medium were quantitatively determined through MTT. As shown in Table 1 , the prepared CDs exhibited different inhibiting abilities to various bacteria.

When using the same chiral source (Glu), the DGU obtained by one-step synthesis can inhibit S. aureus up to 25 μg/mL, which is 4 times higher than the NDGU and 12 times higher than that of NLGU obtained by two-step coupling. This proved the synthesis route set a great impact on the bacterial ability of prepared CDs. When using Cys to replace Glu to prepare DCS and LCS, the antibacterial activity of DGU is 6 times higher than that DCS and 4 times higher than that of LCS. Similarly, DGU shows inhibiting ability to E. coli at 50 μg/mL, at least 4 times higher than that of NDCU, NLGU and D/LCS.

Based on the experiment results, it can be shown that the antibacterial ability of chiral CDs is determined by three aspects: the bacteria type, synthesis route, and raw materials. All six types of prepared CDs showed higher inhibitory ability for G+ than G− bacteria (Scheme 1 .), because the cell walls of G+ bacteria were made of peptidoglycan (PG) and acidic polysaccharides like teichoic acid, but the cell walls of G− bacteria are more complex and multi-layered structure and have an outer membrane that could work as an osmotic barrier and protection for a series of antibiotics [46].

The produced DGU were also contrasted with other chiral CDs described in the literature (Table 2 ). The findings demonstrated that DGU had considerably more antibacterial activity against E. coli than did various other chiral CDs, and the manufacturing was easier.

We discuss the reasons for the antimicrobial variability of the prepared chiral CDs. As shown in the experimental results, the antibacterial activity of the CDs prepared by one-step method was superior to that of the CDs prepared by two-step method. This may be because, in one-step synthesis, PEI1800 was synthesized together with amino acids as raw materials, and the presence of residual amino groups both on the surface of the CDs, and a large amounts of amino group residues made their antibacterial ability greatly enhanced [47]. In contrast, for the CDs synthesized by two-step method, although PEI1800 was used as a raw material in the first step of CDs synthesis, the coupling process caused the amino groups to be wrapped outside the surface of the CDs, reducing the number of exposed amino groups and thus the antibacterial ability was significantly reduced. This was also confirmed in the zeta potential (Fig. 7 ), where both the DGU (+7.09 mV) and LGU (+2.38 mV) synthesized in one-step had a better positive charge and easily bound to negatively charged bacteria [48].

Because the MurA enzyme is the rate-limiting enzyme of the first step reaction in the synthesis of PG in the bacterial cell wall [49], and our synthesized D/LGU with Glu residues could specifically bind to MurA enzyme and interfere with PG synthesis, we were able to achieve the antibacterial effect. The D/LCS with Cys residues does not have the corresponding binding ability, so the antibacterial effect of DGU/LGU synthesized from Glu is much better than that of DCS/LCS. The nature of the amino acids and their uses, therefore, cause significant variances in their antibacterial effectiveness even when they are produced using the same synthetic process.

To further explore the antibacterial mechanism of chiral CDs, we used S. aureus as a model bacterium and observed the morphological changes of S. aureus by SEM. Fig. 8 shows the SEM images of S. aureus treated with PBS and DGU. The cells of S. aureus without DGU treatment (Fig. 8(a)) were round with smooth surfaces without folds and breaks. And after the treatment of DGU (1 mg/mL), it was observed that the bacterial cell surface were ruptured, severely damaged, and then died (Fig. 8(b)). This also indicates that our chiral CDs can achieve the antibacterial purpose by disrupting the integrity of their cell membranes.

Due to the obvious merits of amino acids like low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and structure diversity, the researchers have shown great interest in their practical applications in antibacterial field [50]. For example, γ-Poly (glutamic acid) has been regarded as a natural fungicide [51], but its low solubility leads to poor antibacterial activity. The prepared CDs with Glu as raw material solve the problems of solubility and stability, and also improve their antibacterial ability.

PG determines the morphology of bacterial cells and also protects the bacteria. Its biosynthesis can be divided into three stages [52], and MurA enzyme plays an important role as the rate-limiting enzyme in the first stage of PG synthesis of the bacterial cell wall [49]. In the first stage, after the formation of N-Acetyl-glucosamine 1-phosphate and Uridine dipho-sphate N-acetylglucosamine (UDP-N-acetylglucosamine) in the cytoplasm, with the catalysis of MurA enzyme, the enolpyruvate group on PEP is transferred onto UDP-N-acetylglucosamine, and forms UDP-N-acetylglucosamine-enolpyruvic acid. Therefore, the inhibition of MurA enzyme's effect could interfere with the formation of PG, inhibit the synthesis of the cell wall and finally achieve the antibacterial result. The four Glu-containing CDs prepared by our lab realize their antibacterial effect through their interaction with cell walls to interfere with the formation of PG.

The PET26b-E.coli was successfully prepared in our lab, and the purified MurA enzyme was obtained. The inhibiting effects of different CDs upon MurA enzyme were investigated. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the inhibiting rate of DGU to MurA enzyme reached 55% at the concentration of 1250 μg/mL, showing excellent inhibiting ability, while for NLGU and NDGU prepared through two-step coupling route, their suppression rate reached only 25% and 26% under the same concentration, which is in accordance with the results of MIC. When using Fosfomycin sodium to determine its inhibitory effect on MurA enzyme (Fig. 9(b)), it was discovered that its inhibiting rate was only 24% at the concentration of 80 μmol/L, much lower than that of prepared D/LGU, proving that the excellent antibacterial effect of prepared D/LGU was realized through interfering the formation of an enzyme which is necessary for the cell wall. This is a new antibacterial target in the field of antibacterial applications and would be widely used in the future.

The molecular conformation of the synthesized CDs has been constructed by the presence of amino acid residues on the surface of the chiral CDs, by infrared mapping as shown in Fig. S4. The results of molecular docking showed (Table 3 ) that the Total Scores of the activity of D/LGU as inhibitors (as a prediction of pIC50) were all above 5 and that DGU were more capable of binding MurA enzymes, which is also consistent with the antibacterial results we obtained.

Fig. 10 clearly shows the interaction pattern of the two glutamate CDs with MurA enzymes. In Fig. 10(c), (d), D/LGU and MurA enzyme binding patterns are similar, and both have hydrogen bonding interactions with ARG91, ARG120, SER162, and VAL163. This result shows that the difference in antibacterial activity between DGU and LGU is not significant and is consistent with the results of MIC experiments. The difference between the two CDs is that there is a hydrogen bonding interaction between DGU also and VAL161, one between LGU and LYS164. This difference is apparently caused by the difference in the spatial position of the groups between DGU and LGU due to the difference in stereo-configuration.

In summary, six chiral CDs have been produced using distinct starting materials (cysteine and glutamic acid) in both one step and two step processes, and the products have been characterized by multiple means. The research showed that D/LGU prepared by one-step route exhibited better antibacterial ability than the other four CDs with the MIC of DGU against S. aureus at 25 μg/mL, 4–12 times stronger than that of NDGU/NLGU and 4–6 times stronger than that of D/LCS. MurA enzyme inhibition experiments were carried out on D/LGU, together with SEM images, and the results showed that D/LGU realizes antibacterial behavior through inhibiting MurA enzyme during the PG synthesis to interrupt the formation of PG, and finally breaking the cell membrane of bacterial cells. In addition, the prepared chiral CDs also exhibit excellent biocompatibility, showing great potential in applications like detection, antibacterial, biological application, and fluorescent labeling.

Dan Zhao: Writing – original draft, Writing-review, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Mengyu Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Kang Dai: Software. Huan Liu: Investigation. Yan Jiao: Project administration, Resources. Xincai Xiao: Conceptualization, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978329) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of South-Central Minzu University (CZY22003).
